retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Phenobarbital is rapidly absorbed with a time-to-peak concentration of 2 to 4 hours. The bioavailability of phenobarbital is approximately 90% in adults and less in neonatal populations. [29] Sodium salts of barbiturates are absorbed more rapidly, and food delays the onset of action of orally administered barbiturates. The IV route is usually reserved for managing status epilepticus (phenobarbital sodium) or the induction or maintenance of general anesthesia (eg, methohexital or thiopental). Thiopental observes first-order kinetics in low doses of 5 mg/kg boluses. In high-dose or prolonged infusions, non-linear kinetics will occur due to the progressive saturation of enzyme systems. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Highly lipid-soluble barbiturates cross the blood-brain barrier rapidly, but rapid redistribution from the CNS to peripheral tissues occurs. [28] Thiopental and methohexital undergo rapid redistribution after IV injections into less-vascular tissues, particularly muscle and fat, decreasing the concentration of barbiturates in the plasma and brain. Terminating the effect of a single bolus IV injection of thiopental occurs by redistribution from the central compartment to peripheral compartments. [31]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Thiopental: When using the IV route of administration in pediatrics, wide variation in the required dose is noted. Cote recommended a higher dose range for unpremeditated children. [41] Thiopental was removed from the United States market in 2011 primarily based on the diversion of the drug within the United States as a lethal injection drug. [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Concerning the short-acting IV barbiturates, methohexital was found in breastmilk at the highest concentrations 1 to 2 hours after an IV dose and undetectable 24 to 48 hours after an IV dose. [51] For thiopental, when used for induction of anesthesia for cesarean section, the highest concentration of thiopental in breast milk was in the first nursing after anesthesia, about 0.9 mg/L. Breastfeeding can be resumed as soon as the mother has recovered sufficiently from general anesthesia. However, if a combination of drugs is used during the procedure, follow the standard operating procedure considering the elimination half-life of each agent. [52]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Administration -- Adult Dosage
retrieved_docs.content: Methohexital: Methohexital and thiopental may be administered rectally in pediatrics, particularly if the child cannot cooperate with IV needle administration. This method is best suited in procedures of short duration, such as radiology or dentistry. The usual dose for methohexital is 1 to 1.5 mg/kg for induction. Barbiturates administered for the induction of anesthesia in adults are most commonly given as an IV bolus for a rapid and pleasant loss of consciousness. [39] [40]

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Administration -- Pharmacodynamics and Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: After a single bolus or short-term infusion, the termination of action is primarily by redistribution, owing to the lipophilicity of the drug. Metabolism and excretion play minor roles in the termination of effects following a single bolus. Prolonged infusions may lead to slower emergence from anesthesia once the tissues and blood have reached a steady-state concentration. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Barbiturates are a category of sedative-hypnotic medications used for treating seizure disorders, neonatal withdrawal, insomnia, preoperative anxiety, and the induction of coma to address increased intracranial pressure (ICP). In addition, these medications are also helpful for inducing anesthesia. Thiopental was introduced in 1934 for general anesthesia induction. [1] This drug was the predominant intravenous (IV) anesthetic induction agent until propofol replaced it. [2] In refractory status epilepticus, a Cochrane Review found that thiopental was equally effective as propofol for controlling seizure activity. [3] In 2011, the primary supplier of thiopental chose to cease its production due to its involvement in lethal injection procedures, resulting in its unavailability in the United States. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Steady State Concentration -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The concept of constant state is crucial in specific clinical settings, such as general anesthesia. Particularly, in totally intravenous anesthesia (TIVA) and targeted-controlled infusion (TCI) approaches, the drugs' kinetics, such as those of propofol, and remifentanil, are used to maintain a constant blood concentration and, therefore, a  predictable concentration at the site effector (brain).

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturate Toxicity -- Toxicokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Ultra–short-acting barbiturates (eg, thiopental and methohexital) are given intravenously and used as anesthetics. Due to their ultra–short duration of effect, these drugs are rarely misused. [23] Short-acting and intermediate-acting barbiturates (eg, pentobarbital and secobarbital) have durations of effect of 2 to 6 hours and are used as sleeping aids and sedatives. [23] These drugs have been the most frequently misused and are the most common barbiturate agents that contribute to substance use disorder. [23] Long-acting barbiturates (eg, phenobarbital) have durations of effect of greater than 6 hours and are used as antiepileptic agents and in the treatment of alcohol withdrawal. Owing to their longer durations of effect, these agents are not misused as frequently as short- and intermediate-acting agents. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Isoflurane -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Distribution: Isoflurane has a blood–gas partition coefficient of 1.4 and a minimum alveolar concentration (MAC) of approximately 1.2%. Metabolism: Isoflurane is metabolized in the liver by CYP2E1 to trifluoroacetylated metabolites. They are capable of causing hepatotoxicity. Elimination: Isoflurane is almost entirely eliminated via exhalation. [9] [10] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Administration -- Pharmacodynamics and Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Duration of action: An induction dose of propofol will have a clinical effect for approximately 10 minutes. The prolonged or repeated administration will accumulate in peripheral tissues and will cause an increased duration of action. Distribution: Large volume of distribution. Protein Binding: 97% to 99%. Metabolism: Hepatic oxidation and conjugation to sulfate and glucuronide conjugates. Clearance: Hepatic clearance is approximately 60%, with extrahepatic clearance making up the remaining 40%, most of which is via the kidneys.

retrieved_docs.title: Local Anesthetic Drugs Used In Dentistry -- Summary / Explanation -- Articaine
retrieved_docs.content: Articaine has an efficient pattern of clearance. Once absorbed into the bloodstream from the injection site, articaine is distributed throughout body tissues. The thiophene ring contains an ester side chain inactivated by plasma esterases in the blood. [9] Articaine has a half-life of around 20 minutes, which is much shorter than lidocaine and other amides hepatically metabolized.

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Administration -- Pharmacodynamics and Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Route of Administration: Intravenous Onset of action: Propofol has a rapid onset of action that is dose-dependent and less than a minute.

retrieved_docs.title: Atracurium -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Absorption: Atracurium has an onset of action of approximately 2 minutes when an intubating dose is given. This medication is classified as an intermediate-acting, non-depolarizing muscle relaxant with a duration of action of approximately 40 to 45 minutes. The elimination half-life is approximately 20 minutes. In older patients, the half-life may increase by approximately 15% due primarily to decreased clearance. [14] Distribution: The volume of distribution is approximately 160 mL/kg, and the plasma protein binding is 82%.

retrieved_docs.title: Propofol -- Administration -- Pharmacodynamics and Pharmacokinetics
retrieved_docs.content: Half-life: Propofol is bi-phasic, with its initial half-life being relatively quick, around 40 minutes, and its terminal half-life usually being 4 to 7 hours. Context-sensitive half-time may be up to 1 to 3 days after a 10-day infusion. The clinical effect of propofol is much shorter in duration. Excretion: Primarily renal.

retrieved_docs.title: Minimum Alveolar Concentration -- Issues of Concern -- Physiological Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Factors such as species, sex, hypothyroidism, hyperthyroidism, or duration of anesthesia do not affect MAC; however, the age of the patient does influence the minimum alveolar concentration. MAC peaks at 6 months of age, and then decreases by approximately 6% per decade, regardless of volatile anesthetic. [5] A comprehensive meta-analysis determined the relationship between MAC and age could be described with reasonable accuracy by the following equation: MACage = MAC40 × 10^ [-0.00269(age - 40)], where MACage is the MAC at a given age and MAC40 is the MAC value at age 40. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse group of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. The category “anesthetics, general” includes chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, and propofol as child agents, each representing a distinct chemical class with specific pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic profiles. In addition, xenon—a noble gas—also falls under the classification of general anesthetics, indicating that it shares the core functional purpose of inducing general anesthesia despite its unique mechanism of action. Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of substances that can be employed to achieve controlled, reversible unconsciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 394
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulators are characterized as antagonists and inhibitory agents that can block or diminish GABA‑mediated neurotransmission. This pharmacologic profile encompasses a broad spectrum of compounds, including the volatile anesthetic gases chloroform, enflurane, halothane, isoflurane and nitrous oxide; the intravenous anesthetics etomidate, methohexital, propofol and bupivacaine; the barbiturates flurazepam and flurothyl; the dissociative agent fencyclidine; the hypnotic‑sedative chloralose; the carboxylic acids; the inert gas nitrous oxide; the mineral kaolin; as well as amitriptyline, thiorphan and other hypnotic‑sedative agents that are listed as allowed qualifiers for antagonist‑inhibitory actions. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in selecting drugs for anesthesia, sedation, analgesia and neuropsychiatric treatment and in anticipating therapeutic effects, drug interactions and adverse outcomes. Antagonists and inhibitors are frequently qualified by local‑anesthetic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse class of pharmacologic agents used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. This class includes chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these agents functions as a “child” of the broader category of general anesthetics, reflecting their role as specific drugs that share the common purpose of producing and maintaining general anesthesia. While all serve the same overarching clinical goal, they differ in chemical structure, mechanism of action, potency, onset and duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, allowing clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to the individual patient’s physiological status, surgical requirements, and risk factors. Understanding the full spectrum of these agents enhances patient safety, optimizes peri‑operative management, and supports informed selection of the most appropriate anesthetic regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1326
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics comprise a diverse group of agents used to induce loss of consciousness and analgesia during surgical procedures. Specific agents that fall under this category include chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol, each representing a distinct child compound within the broader class of general anesthetics. While these agents vary in chemical structure, pharmacokinetic profile, and clinical indications, they all share the primary function of providing safe and effective general anesthesia for patients undergoing operative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 460
retrieved_docs.content: GABA‑modulating agents are linked to hypnotic, sedative, and general anesthetic effects, indicating that drugs acting on the GABAergic system can produce sedation, hypnosis, and reversible loss of consciousness. Specific compounds classified as hypnotics and sedatives—including the combination aspirin / butalbital / caffeine, chloralose, etomidate, flurazepam, hexobarbital, and halothane—belong to the broader parent class of CNS‑depressant substances (“látky tlumící činnost CNS”). General anesthetics, a subset of CNS depressants, comprise a diverse group of inhalational and intravenous agents such as chloralose, chloroform, enflurane, etomidate, fencyclidine, flurothyl, halothane, hexobarbital, methohexital, nitrous oxide, propofol, and the noble gas xenon. Xenon exemplifies how chemically distinct agents can function as reversible general anesthetics, while flurazepam exemplifies a GABA modulator that enhances inhibitory neurotransmission. Together, these relationships illustrate the overlapping pharmacologic landscape in which GABA‑modulating hypnotics/sedatives and a wide array of general anesthetics intersect within CNS‑depressant therapy and peri‑operative patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a broad spectrum of agents that are classified under the category “anesthetics, general.” This group includes the inhalational agents chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, and flurothyl, as well as the intravenous agents methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is identified as a specific type of general anesthetic. Together, these substances represent the diverse pharmacologic options used to achieve reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia during surgical and procedural care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a broad class of agents used to induce reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. Within this class, chloroform, enfluran (enflurane), fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran (isoflurane), methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol are recognized as specific child agents that share the overarching function of producing general anesthesia. Xenon is also classified as a type of general anesthetic, reflecting its role as an inhalational agent with anesthetic properties. Although these agents differ in chemical structure, potency, onset and recovery times, and side‑effect profiles, they are all integrated into the therapeutic repertoire for achieving controlled, reversible unconsciousness in patients undergoing operative or diagnostic interventions. Understanding the relationships among these individual anesthetic agents and the general anesthetic category supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies to optimize patient safety and perioperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2045
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthetics, general constitute a broad class of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. This class includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol, each of which is a specific child of the general anesthetic category. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic. Together, these diverse compounds—ranging from volatile liquids such as chloroform and isofluran to intravenous agents like propofol and ketamin, and to gaseous agents such as nitrous oxide and xenon—provide clinicians with multiple options for tailoring anesthetic care to the physiological needs, safety considerations, and procedural requirements of individual patients. Understanding the relationships among these agents supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies in peri‑operative medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a diverse group of agents that are used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical and diagnostic procedures. The class of general anesthetics includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol. Each of these agents has distinct pharmacologic characteristics, such as differences in onset speed, duration of action, potency, and side‑effect profile, which influence their selection for specific clinical scenarios. Understanding the full spectrum of general anesthetic agents—ranging from the historic inhalational agent chloroform to the widely used intravenous agent propofol—allows clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to individual patient needs, procedural requirements, and safety considerations, thereby optimizing peri‑operative care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2243
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a class of drugs used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical procedures, and they are collectively referred to in Arabic as “التخدير العام.” This category includes a diverse group of agents that are considered “children” of the general anesthetic class, such as chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic, illustrating that both traditional volatile compounds (e.g., chloroform, enflurane, isoflurane) and newer agents (e.g., propofol, methohexital, xenon) belong to the same therapeutic group. Understanding that these substances share the common purpose of producing general anesthesia helps clinicians select appropriate agents based on pharmacologic properties, safety profiles, and specific patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Methoxyfluran, a halogenated ether anesthetic, is recognized for its ability to induce فقدان الوعي (loss of consciousness), which is a central therapeutic objective in the administration of general anesthesia. By depressing central nervous system activity, methoxyfluran reliably produces the unconscious state required for surgical procedures, highlighting its role as an effective agent for achieving controlled loss of consciousness in clinical practice.

Collectively, this diverse group of opioid analgesics illustrates the breadth of pharmacologic options available for effective pain control across different clinical settings.

Several of these agents—chloralose, chloroform, enflurane, etomidate and fencyclidine—are also identified as “child” general anesthetic compounds, illustrating the overlap between GABA‑modulating activity, hypnotic‑sedative effects and general anesthesia. Within the neurotransmitter‑modulation hierarchy, flurazepam is linked to GABA modulators by an inverse‑isa relationship, indicating that flurazepam exhibits properties opposite to typical GABA modulators. Similarly, racecadotril is connected to the class of carboxylic acids by an inverse‑isa link and is mapped from thiorphan, suggesting a structural or functional correspondence between these two compounds. Finally, quinoline derivatives and related antitrematodal substances are placed within the praziquantel family, situating these agents among the broader pharmacologic context of praziquantel‑derived antitrematodals. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1394
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulators are closely linked to the class of hypnotics and sedatives, reflecting the central role of GABA‑ergic mechanisms in producing sleep‑inducing and calming effects. Several specific agents—chloralose, etomidate, flurazepam, and hexobarbital—are each classified as hypnotics and sedatives, and halothane is also related to this pharmacologic group. The broader category of hypnotics and sedatives is situated within the methapyrilen family of compounds, indicating a shared chemical lineage. Notably, flurazepam, a benzodiazepine, functions as a GABA modulator, exemplifying how modulation of the GABA system underlies many hypnotic and sedative drugs. Understanding these interconnections helps clinicians appreciate the mechanisms of action, therapeutic uses, and potential cross‑reactivity among agents used for sedation and anesthesia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 53
retrieved_docs.content: classifications, specific active‑ingredient combinations, dosing strengths, delivery methods, excipient roles, and critical drug‑interaction contraindications to optimize pain‑management regimens, maximize analgesic efficacy, and mitigate risks for individual patients.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2029
retrieved_docs.content: Local anesthetics are a pharmacologic class employed to achieve reversible loss of sensation in a specific region of the body. Within this class, mepivakain, prilokain, and propoxykain are identified as child agents of the broader local anesthetic category, while bupivakain and etidokain are also classified as local anesthetics. Recognizing that mepivakain, prilokain, propoxykain, bupivakain, and etidokain share the same fundamental mechanism of action enables clinicians to select appropriate agents for regional blocks, infiltration, or topical anesthesia and to appreciate their related pharmacologic properties, including variations in potency, duration of effect, and safety profile.
